quote: Is there someone else in the home who maybe would've filled it out?
stewart_read: When Justin denies the form, Meg probes for another household member. The alternative hypothesis — that this is a strategic indirect legitimacy attempt, trying to find a path back to a valid lead premise — is worth considering. But the evidence cuts against it: Meg doesn't follow the probe with any reframe of the call's purpose, and when Justin says no, she simply moves on. The more supported read is reactive: Meg is trying to preserve the lead premise because she has no legitimacy-recovery move ready. A strategic probe would have been followed by a pivot ('Either way, since I have you — can I take 30 seconds to tell you why I'm calling?'). That pivot never comes.
alternative_hypothesis: The household-member probe is a strategic indirect legitimacy attempt — Meg is trying to find a valid lead anchor before pivoting. This is the stronger of the two reads: it's a recognizable move, not pure flailing.
coaching_implication: Manager should ask Meg: 'What's your move when someone denies the form?' She likely doesn't have one. Drill the small-ask pivot from protocols.hello_objection_not_interested — 'Can I have 30 seconds to tell you why I'm calling?' — as the standard legitimacy-recovery move when the form premise collapses.

quote: Well, have you ever looked at solar? Do you know much about the program itself?
stewart_read: This is the root-cause moment of the call. Meg skips the verify section entirely and pivots to interest-probing. The first required verify item — confirm address, home type, ownership — would have surfaced Justin's renter status in under 60 seconds. Instead, Meg spends the next 105 seconds pitching an ineligible lead. The skip is not strategic; there is no later verify attempt. It is a clean protocol omission.
coaching_implication: This is the primary coaching beat. Manager should replay 00:58 and ask: 'What should have come next?' The answer is the verify section — specifically homeownership confirmation. Meg needs to internalize verify as the first move after any legitimacy challenge, not interest-probing. The homeownership miss at 02:44 is a direct consequence of this skip.

stewart_read: Justin discloses financial hardship directly and plainly. Meg's response — 'that's one of the nice things about our program here is there's nothing upfront or out of pocket to go solar' — contains zero empathy acknowledgment before the pitch pivot. The line 'Nah. I feel you there.' at 01:11 is Justin's own voice, not Meg's; Meg offers no equivalent. This is a hard empathy miss: a vulnerability disclosure met with an immediate product feature. Even on a call that was already heading toward DQ, the absence of a human acknowledgment before the pivot is a trust-destroying move.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg's pivot to 'nothing upfront or out of pocket' could be read as a strategic objection_inversion — using the financial concern as the entry point for the value proposition. But the inversion requires at least a brief acknowledgment to land; without it, it reads as dismissal, not reframe.
coaching_implication: Manager should isolate 01:09–01:14 and ask Meg to replay it with a one-sentence acknowledgment before the pitch: something like 'That makes total sense — that's actually why a lot of people look at this.' The codex's empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure principle applies even on short calls heading toward DQ. The habit of acknowledging before pivoting is what the manager is building.

quote: I cannot pay even. I cannot pay even. Just for free.
stewart_read: Justin repeats his financial distress with more urgency — 'I cannot pay even. I cannot pay even.' This is a second vulnerability disclosure, escalating the first. Meg's response is to clarify that it's not free and explain the bill-replacement model. The explanation is technically accurate but again contains no empathy beat. At this point Justin has disclosed financial hardship twice in 12 seconds and received two consecutive pitch pivots. The pattern compounds the trust deficit.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg's clarification ('it's not free, it replaces your bill') is necessary — Justin has a factual misunderstanding that would derail any further conversation. The strategic read is that she's correcting the premise before building on it. But the correction and the empathy beat are not mutually exclusive; the miss is the absence of the latter, not the presence of the former.
coaching_implication: Pair this moment with 01:09 in the coaching session. The pattern is two consecutive vulnerability disclosures met with two consecutive pitch pivots. Manager should help Meg see that acknowledging the pain ('I hear you — that's exactly the situation this is designed for') before the explanation is what makes the explanation land rather than bounce.

quote: Just just just just give me a rough idea before even I I go ahead. Like, how much usually people pay on a monthly basis?
stewart_read: Justin asks for a ballpark monthly payment — a direct price-before-design request. Meg's response correctly declines to give a number and explains the sun-study / home-specific design rationale. The strategy is schema-aligned (redirect to design). However, the explanation runs roughly 20-25 seconds and includes technical detail (sun hours, sun study, kilowatt measurement) that is closer-tier content. The redirect is correct; the length and technical depth are scope creep.
coaching_implication: The redirect is the right move. Coach Meg to tighten it: the explanation should be one sentence ('It's completely home-specific — that's why we build a design first') before moving to the next verify item. The sun-study detail is not needed at setter stage and adds time on a call that was already losing energy.

quote: I'm I'm I'm renting. Who who told you that I have a house?
stewart_read: The hard DQ surfaces at 02:44 — nearly three minutes into the call. This is a direct consequence of Meg skipping the verify section at 00:58. The renter status is the first required verify item and would have ended the call cleanly in under 60 seconds. Justin's agitation ('who who told you') signals that the delay has cost trust as well as time. Meg's terminal handling — acknowledging the form, offering to remove him — is correct and clean. The failure is entirely upstream.
coaching_implication: Use this moment as the payoff in the coaching session: 'This is what happens when we skip verify.' The manager should walk the timeline — 00:58 skip → 01:14 pitch to ineligible lead → 02:44 DQ — so Meg can see the causal chain. The goal is not to shame the outcome but to make the verify-first habit feel protective, not procedural.
